notes.anchor: The book of his on Cato, which he sent to me
notes.note: Cicero had written a laudatory pamphlet, the Cato, praising Cato Uticensis after his suicide at Utica in 46 BC; Hirtius (and later Caesar himself in the Anticato) replied with attacks. Cicero wants Hirtius's hostile book circulated, confident its abuse will only set off the dead Cato's glory the more.

notes.anchor: As for what you are doing through Mustela
notes.note: Mustela (Tamisius) was an agent connected with the estate Cicero hoped to buy, a co-surety on the Faberian bond and useful in securing a buyer's access to the heirs. Cicero treats him as a reliable go-between in the gardens negotiation.

notes.anchor: ever since the time of Pontianus
notes.note: An otherwise unknown acquaintance through whom Cicero's friendship with Mustela had begun; the reference is too compressed to identify the man or the affair securely.

notes.anchor: Pompey is not being held at Carteia
notes.note: This "Pompey" is Cn. Pompeius the younger, elder son of Pompey the Great (long dead), who after his defeat by Caesar at Munda in March 45 BC fled to Carteia, a port near Gibraltar. The rumour that he was trapped there proved false, and Cicero is anxious that a fresh round of the Spanish war is still to come.

Fulviniaster, Cicero's coinage from an otherwise obscure Fulvinius, brands Philotimus as a notorious purveyor of inflated or false news. The diminutive-pejorative suffix (-aster) marks him a second-rate, would-be version of the type.

A current piece of news about a man named Caninius that Cicero wants explained; which of several contemporary Caninii is meant cannot be fixed with certainty. The remark shows how the grieving Cicero still followed the day's gossip from Rome.
